Sedation for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy: ESGE and ESGENA Guideline
  • Source: Endoscopy, Vol. 42, Published online: 2026-07-21
  • Topic: Sedation practices for gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures
  • Author(s): Triantafyllou K, et al. (28 authors from European institutions)
1. Pre-Endoscopic Assessment
1.1 Informed Consent for Sedated GI Procedures
  • Informed consent --> must include sedation coverage
  • ESGE Position Statement outlines best practice requirements
  • Local legislation determines need for separate consent form
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
1.2 Patient Risk Factors for Sedation-Related Adverse Events
  • High-risk patient criteria:
  • ASA score III–IV --> ↑ risk of sedation-related AEs
  • Mallampati class III–IV --> ↑ airway complications
  • Elderly (>75 years) --> 46% more AEs than age <66
  • BMI ≥35 kg/m² --> 27% more AEs than BMI <21
  • Emergency endoscopy --> 11% more AEs than routine
  • Pre-assessment components:
  • Safety checklist implementation
  • Clinical examination and patient history
  • ASA score evaluation (not always accurate)
  • Comorbidities assessment: cardiovascular, chronic lung disease/apnea, chronic kidney/liver disease
  • ECG and spirometry on indication
  • Risk mitigation strategies:
  • Enhanced periprocedural monitoring for high-risk patients
  • Specific sedation regimen tailored to risk profile
  • Consider unsedated endoscopy or newer sedatives (e.g., remimazolam)
1.2.1 GLP-1 Receptor Agonist Considerations
  • GLP-1RAs impair GI motility --> delayed gastric emptying
  • ↑ retention of gastric contents during upper endoscopy
  • Meta-analyses show: no significant difference in aspiration rates (NNT 794 to observe one event)
  • Management --> individualized, shared decision-making
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
1.3 Procedure Risk Factors
  • High-complexity procedures requiring enhanced monitoring:
  • Upper/lower GI polypectomy/resection of neoplasia
  • Procedures >60 minutes duration
  • Endoscopic bariatric surgery
  • Large variceal bleed with significant gastric contents
  • Foreign body retrieval
  • Therapeutic EUS or ERCP
  • Pancreatic fluid collection drainage
  • Small-bowel device-assisted enteroscopy (DAE)
  • Every hour prolongation --> 41% increase in overall AEs
  • Addition of opioids to propofol --> 43% ↑ AEs; addition of benzodiazepines --> 51% ↑ AEs
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2. Type of Sedation Options
2.1 Diagnostic Colonoscopy Without Sedation
  • Benefits:
  • No compromise on cecal intubation rate, adenoma miss rate, or ADR
  • Patient can resume daily activities immediately
  • Considerations:
  • May require advanced technical skills
  • Water intubation techniques minimize discomfort
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2.2 Unsedated Transnasal/Ultrathin Gastroscopy
  • Ultrathin transnasal endoscopy --> comparable sensitivity/specificity to conventional gastroscopy for esophageal lesions
  • Higher patient satisfaction reported
  • Entire upper GI tract can be examined without reducing lesion detection rates
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2.3 Sedation Provider: NAAP vs. Anesthesiologist
  • NAAP (non-anesthesiologist administration of propofol):
  • Propofol administered by endoscopist or trained nurses
  • Lower doses used (251 mg vs. 340 mg by anesthesiologists)
  • Similar hypoxia rates: NAAP 0.13% vs. AP 0.14%
  • Higher airway intervention rates with anesthesiologist administration
  • Safety outcomes in low-risk (ASA I–II) patients:
  • Major AEs: 0.00% for nonadvanced procedures; 0.05% for advanced
  • Non-major AEs: 11.60% (nonadvanced); 14.74% (advanced)
  • Respiratory AEs: 1.26% (nonadvanced); 5.28% (advanced)
  • Colonoscopy outcomes: No difference in ADR or cecal intubation rate between NAAP and AP
  • TCI (target-controlled infusion) vs. bolus propofol:
  • TCI --> lower hypoxia rate (7.02% vs. 21.59%)
  • TCI --> lower hypotension rate (14.19% vs. 27.06%)
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2.4 Pharmacological Sedation Options
2.4.1 Backbone Sedatives: Propofol and Midazolam
  • Propofol:
  • First-line recommendation (depends on country resources/legislation)
  • Rapid onset, brief duration, short recovery
  • Associated with hypotension and hypoxia
  • Midazolam:
  • Recommended in combination with opiates when analgesia required
  • Slower onset, longer recovery vs. propofol
  • Similar cardiorespiratory outcomes to propofol in meta-analysis of 9 RCTs
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2.4.2 Ultrashort-Acting Sedatives: Remimazolam and Ciprofol
  • Remimazolam:
  • Water-soluble benzodiazepine, GABA-A receptor enhancer
  • Broken down by tissue esterases
  • 56% cardiovascular relative risk reduction vs. propofol
  • Lower respiratory complications (RR 0.38) and cardiovascular AEs (RR 0.49)
  • Ciprofol:
  • 2,6-disubstituted phenol analog of propofol
  • Currently marketed only in China
  • Comparison outcomes (remimazolam vs. propofol):
  • Sedation success: RR 0.98–0.99 (similar)
  • Hypotension: RR 0.40
  • Bradycardia: RR 0.46
  • Respiratory AEs: RR 0.44
  • Conditional recommendation for remimazolam in elderly and patients with cardiovascular/respiratory comorbidities
2.4.3 Combination Sedatives/Analgesics
  • Caution recommended when combining ultrashort-acting sedatives with other agents
  • Conditional recommendation, low certainty of evidence
2.4.3.1 Dexmedetomidine + Propofol
  • Significantly fewer hypotension episodes (RR 1.75)
  • Fewer bradycardia episodes (RR 3.44)
  • Lower total respiratory AE rate (RR 0.35)
  • Harms: Delayed discharge readiness
2.4.3.2 Lidocaine + Propofol
  • Reduces propofol consumption (MD –42.93 mg)
  • Shortens awakening time (MD –3.38 minutes)
  • Reduces post-procedural pain scores
  • Lower respiratory AE rate (RR 0.48)
2.4.3.3 Etomidate + Propofol
  • Safer in terms of hypoxia (OR 0.16), hypotension (OR 0.05), injection site pain (OR 0.14)
  • Harms: Slightly ↑ recovery time, myoclonus risk (OR 3.07), nausea/vomiting
2.4.3.4 Esketamine + Propofol
  • Fewer hypotension episodes (RR 0.46)
  • Lower bradycardia and respiratory AE rates (not significant)
  • Caution: Psychotomimetic effects possible
2.4.3.5 Short-Acting Opioids + Sedatives
  • Alfentanil + propofol: Lower hypotension (RR 0.23), respiratory depression (RR 0.37)
  • Remimazolam + opioid: Lower respiratory depression (RR 0.41), hypotension (RR 0.43)
  • Ciprofol + opioid: Lower hypotension (OR 0.48), bradycardia (OR 0.66)
2.4.4 Anesthetic Throat Spray (Xylocaine)
  • No routine use recommended during gastroscopy under deep sedation
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2.4.5 Antispasmodics During Colonoscopy
  • Routine use not suggested during sedated colonoscopy
  • No reduction in propofol dose or procedure time
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
2.5 Nonpharmacological Adjuncts to Sedation
  • Virtual reality and music:
  • Significantly lower pain (MD –1.02)
  • ↓ patient anxiety levels (MD –1.07)
  • ↑ patient satisfaction (MD 1.67)
  • Lower midazolam/analgesia doses (not significant)
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
3. Requirements for Safe Sedation Administration
3.1 Monitoring Personnel
  • Dedicated healthcare professional trained in sedation administration and patient monitoring required
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
3.2 Equipment and Monitoring Standards
3.2.1 Capnography Monitoring
  • Benefits:
  • Significantly reduces hypoxemia incidence (OR 0.61, 95%CI 0.49–0.77)
  • Reduces severe hypoxemia (OR 0.53, 95%CI 0.35–0.81)
  • Benefit greater in high-risk patients and deeper sedation
  • Recommendation: Suggest capnography for higher-risk patients
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
3.2.2 Bispectral Index (BIS) Monitoring
  • No routine recommendation for GI endoscopic procedures with propofol
  • May reduce total propofol dose (SMD –0.15)
  • No significant impact on intraprocedural hypoxia after trial sequential analysis
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
3.2.3 High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC)
  • No routine recommendation for GI endoscopic procedures with propofol
  • HFNC reduces hypoxemia risk (OR 0.12–0.39 across meta-analyses)
  • Benefit restricted to low-risk patients (BMI <30 or ASA I–II)
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
3.3 Reversal Agents
  • Use --> restricted to managing sedation-analgesia adverse events not improving with non-pharmacological intervention
  • Only 0.02% of patients required reversal agents in large study (119,860 patients)
  • Flumazenil effective for paradoxical reactions to midazolam (93.3% success rate)
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
4. Safe Patient Discharge
4.1 Post-Endoscopy Monitoring Requirements
  • Personnel:
  • Trained and qualified staff required until baseline observations return
  • One nurse can effectively monitor 3–4 patients in stage II recovery
  • Equipment:
  • Pulse oximetry and noninvasive blood pressure monitoring minimum requirements
  • Supplemental oxygen availability
  • Cardiorespiratory emergency management capability
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
4.2 Discharge Criteria and Scoring Systems
  • Scoring systems:
  • Aldrete score
  • Modified post-anesthesia discharge scoring system (mPADSS)
  • Implementation benefits:
  • Standardizes discharge process
  • Shortens recovery time
  • Simple to use regardless of physician expertise
  • Components for assessment:
  • Detailed vital signs record
  • Pain levels evaluation
  • Psychomotor performance assessment
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
4.2.1 Medicolegal Responsibility
  • Performing endoscopist holds overall responsibility for safe recovery and discharge
  • May delegate assessment/discharge to trained personnel based on standardized criteria
  • Conditional recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
4.2.3 Patient Information and Escort Requirements
  • Oral and written information provided regarding:
  • Nutrition post-endoscopy
  • Return to work timing
  • Road traffic participation (active and passive)
  • Legal decision-making capacity
  • Emergency contact details for complications/readmission
  • Accompanying person required at discharge for sedated patients
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
5. Special Situations in Sedation
5.1 Emergency GI Endoscopy
  • Definition: Endoscopy ≤6 hours after presentation (GI bleeding, foreign body, cholangitis)
  • Hemodynamically stable patients:
  • Moderate sedation recommended
  • Alternatively, no sedation feasible in cooperative patients
  • High-risk patients (aspiration risk, hemodynamic instability, significant comorbidities):
  • Anesthesiologist administration required
  • Tracheal intubation for airway protection if needed
  • Evidence: No difference in hemostasis success rate (OR 1.19)
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
5.2 Sedation During Pregnancy and Lactation
  • Multidisciplinary consultation required with anesthetic and obstetric teams
  • Patient's choice incorporated into decision-making
  • Considerations:
  • First trimester: teratogenicity concerns (avoid diazepam)
  • Late third trimester/impending delivery: premature delivery risk
  • Evidence: ERCP studies (1–65 patients) showed no pregnancy terminations due to procedure/sedation
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
5.3 Sedation with Mucolytic/Defoaming Premedication
  • No recommendation to withhold sedation in patients premedicated with simethicone, N-acetylcysteine, or pronase
  • Volumes up to 100 ml administered ~30 minutes before procedure
  • Pooled AE analysis: no excess AEs in premedication group (OR 0.55)
  • Strong recommendation, moderate certainty of evidence
6. Training, Quality, and Environmental Impact
6.1 Sedation Training Requirements
  • ESGE–ESGENA curriculum (2013) covers:
  • Levels of sedation-anesthesia understanding
  • Sedation administration during GI endoscopy
  • Complication management
  • Patient comfort and safety focus
  • Impact evidence: Study of 12,000+ procedures showed:
  • No sentinel AEs
  • 5 moderate-risk AEs (0.05%)
  • 18 minor-risk AEs (0.17%)
  • All managed without requiring additional sedation
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
6.2 Quality Improvement Programs
  • Quality domains:
  • Patient safety (preprocedural risk stratification, AE identification/management)
  • Technical outcome (successful completion at planned sedation level)
  • Patient comfort
  • Interventions showing improvement:
  • Auditing and feedback on anesthesiology practices
  • Nursing staff involvement in quality improvement
  • Recommendation: Development and implementation of quality improvement program with performance indicators
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
6.3 Environmental Impact Considerations
  • GI endoscopy and anesthesiology departments --> top waste producers in hospitals
  • Recommendation: Rational use of sedatives to reduce environmental impact
  • ESGE–ESGENA Position Statement supports peri-procedural medication rationalization
  • Strong recommendation, very low certainty of evidence
7. Research Gaps and Future Directions
7.1 Pre-Procedural Risk Stratification
  • Impact of preprocedural risk stratification in high-risk patients (age, comorbidities, BMI)
  • Tailored approaches based on patient-specific factors (anxiety, previous sedation history)
7.2 Sedation Type and Administration
  • Choice based on procedure complexity, setting (ambulatory vs. emergency), administrator type
7.3 New Sedative Agents
  • Evaluation of newer drugs with improved efficacy, shorter recovery, fewer side effects
7.4 Monitoring Standardization
  • Optimal regimens for minimal/moderate/deep sedation and general anesthesia
  • Post-procedure observation and discharge criteria development
7.5 Endoscopy-Specific Discharge Scoring
  • Development of scoring system tailored for GI endoscopy (current systems suboptimal)
  • Evidence on optimal staff-to-patient ratios in recovery
7.6 Post-Procedural Complications
  • Comprehensive risk analysis of late complications (infections, thromboembolic, circulatory events)
7.7 Patient-Reported Experience Measures
  • Development of tools specifically designed to assess quality of sedation during endoscopy
7.8 Artificial Intelligence Integration
  • Identification of most beneficial applications in patient journey (pre-assessment to recovery)
7.9 Environmental Impact Studies
  • Direct comparison of environmental impacts across sedation strategies (no sedation vs. conscious sedation vs. MAC vs. GA)
8. Key Pharmacological Properties Summary
Table: Sedative and Analgesic Drug Pharmacokinetics
Type Medication Onset (seconds) Clinical Effect (minutes)
Sedative Propofol 15–60 3–15
Sedative Ciprofol 30–60 5–10
Sedative Remimazolam 30–120 3–8
Sedative Etomidate 30–60 3–5
Sedative Esk etamine 30–120 15–60
Sedative Midazolam 120–300 20–80
Sedative Dexmedetomidine 180–300 15–30
Analgesic Remifentanil 30–60 2–6
Analgesic Alfentanil 45 5–10
Analgesic Sufentanil 60 30
Analgesic Fentanyl 60–120 30–60
9. Levels of Sedation and Anesthesia
Level Responsiveness Airway Spontaneous Ventilation Cardiovascular
Minimal sedation Normal response to verbal stimuli Not affected Not affected Not affected
Moderate sedation Purposeful response to verbal/tactile stimuli No intervention required Adequate Usually unaffected
Deep sedation Purposeful response after repeated/painful stimuli Intervention may be required May be inadequate Usually unaffected
General anesthesia Unarousable even with painful stimuli Intervention often required Frequently inadequate May be impaired
10. Main Recommendations Summary
Strong Recommendations (Very Low Certainty)
  • Informed consent for GI procedures --> include sedation
  • Pre-assessment and specific regimens for high-risk patients
  • Sedation regimen determined by procedure complexity
  • Sedation provided by dedicated trained healthcare professional
  • Propofol first-line (country-dependent); midazolam + opiates when analgesia required
  • Reversal agents --> restricted to adverse events not improving with non-pharmacological intervention
  • Monitoring until baseline observations return
  • Oral/written post-endoscopy information and escort at discharge
  • Emergency endoscopy: moderate sedation in stable patients; anesthesiologist for high-risk patients
  • Anesthetic/obstetric consultation during pregnancy
  • No routine use of BIS monitoring or HFNC for GI endoscopy with propofol
Conditional Recommendations (Low/Very Low Certainty)
  • GLP-1RA management --> individualized
  • Diagnostic colonoscopy without sedation based on patient/endoscopist preference
  • Remimazolam in elderly and cardiovascular/respiratory comorbidity patients
  • Caution with combining ultrashort-acting sedatives with other agents
  • Capnography monitoring for higher-risk patients
  • Scoring systems and standardized checklists for discharge readiness
No Routine Use Recommendations
  • Anesthetic throat spray (xylocaine) during deep sedated gastroscopy
  • Antispasmodics during sedated colonoscopy
  • Bispectral index (BIS) monitoring routine use
  • High-flow nasal cannula routine use
